structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a regional hospital system spanning 5 years (60 months) and 100,000+ patient visits. Data includes complete visit sequences (admission dates, discharge dates, clinical codes, procedures, medications, labs), demographic attributes (age, sex, socioeconomic status proxy), and 30-day readmission labels.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission = 1 if patient was admitted to the hospital within 30 calendar days of discharge from the index visit; readmission = 0 otherwise. Labels are derived from hospital administrative records (admission timestamps) and are objective and verifiable. Any patient with fewer than 1 prior visit is excluded to ensure sequence-based features are meaningful.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with patient-level stratification. Training set: all visits occurring in months 1–36 (60% of timeline). Validation set: visits in months 37–48 (20% of timeline). Test set: visits in months 49–60 (20% of timeline). Justification: (1) Temporal ordering prevents information leakage from future visits into training; (2) RNNs are sensitive to distribution shift over time, and temporal splits reveal this; (3) Stratification by readmission class within each window ensures balanced class representation across splits given imbalance (~8–12% readmission rate typical in such data). Patient identifiers are respected: all visits from a single patient must fall entirely within one split to prevent patient leakage.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scalers for continuous clinical features, encoders for categorical codes, vocabulary builders for visit sequences) are fit exclusively on the training set. The fitted transformers are then applied deterministically to validation and test sets without re-fitting. This prevents the validation/test sets from influencing feature normalization or encoding choices.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets constructed in parallel for fair comparison: (1) Logistic regression baseline: demographic features (age, sex, encoded socioeconomic status proxy), clinical summary features (comorbidity count via ICD-10 codes, number of chronic conditions, prior 12-month hospitalization count, emergency department visit frequency, medication count), and visit-level features (length of stay, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis code encoded via clinical domain knowledge). (2) RNN model: complete visit sequence representation consisting of the preceding 12 months of visits (or all available history if <12 months), where each visit is encoded as: [admission date, discharge date, LOS, primary/secondary diagnosis codes, procedure codes, medication classes, lab value summaries]. Sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed length of 12 visits. Both models receive identical demographic/clinical context features as input or concatenated embeddings to ensure fair comparison.
structured_choices.model: Primary model: LSTM-based RNN with (1) embedding layers for diagnosis/procedure/medication codes (embedding dimension: 64–128), (2) two stacked LSTM layers (64–128 hidden units each) with dropout (0.3–0.5), (3) attention mechanism over the sequence dimension to weight informative visits, (4) dense layers (64 units, ReLU) on the concatenated [attention output + demographic features], (5) sigmoid output for binary classification. Hyperparameters selected via validation set performance. Baseline model: Logistic regression trained on the same demographic and clinical feature set, with L2 regularization tuned via validation set AUC.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression using identical demographic and clinical features (comorbidity counts, admission counts, LOS distributions, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis) available in the EHR, excluding raw visit sequences. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter search over regularization strength (C parameter) via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set, evaluated on the validation set. The baseline is fair because: (1) both models use the same label and split strategy, (2) both receive the same demographic confounders, (3) the baseline uses established clinical features that are interpretable and commonly used in readmission prediction, and (4) the baseline receives hyperparameter optimization to avoid strawman comparisons.
structured_choices.primary_metric: ROC-AUC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve). Justification: (1) Directly aligns with the hospital's objective of identifying high-risk patients for intervention; a higher AUC means the model better ranks patients by readmission risk, enabling the hospital to apply limited resources (discharge planning, follow-up calls) to those most likely to be readmitted; (2) ROC-AUC is robust to class imbalance (readmission ~8–12%), unlike accuracy; (3) AUC does not require choosing a specific probability threshold, which the hospital can tune later for operational constraints (sensitivity vs. specificity trade-off); (4) operationally, the hospital can set a threshold on predicted probability to target a specific recall or precision, and AUC provides the foundation for this flexibility.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC: provides a complementary view when class imbalance is severe; summarizes the trade-off between true positive rate and positive predictive value.
Sensitivity at 90% specificity: operationally relevant; indicates the true positive rate when the model is tuned to minimize false alarms.
Calibration error (Brier score, calibration plots): measures whether predicted probabilities reflect true frequencies; critical if the hospital uses absolute risk scores for decision-making.
F1-score at the validation-optimized threshold: provides a single-number summary of balanced performance.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: The validation set (visits in months 37–48) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. For the RNN: embedding dimension, LSTM hidden size, dropout rate, and learning rate are tuned by training on the training set and evaluating on the validation set, selecting hyperparameters that maximize ROC-AUC. For logistic regression: regularization strength C is tuned similarly. No model selection occurs on the test set; the test set is reserved for final evaluation only.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (visits in months 49–60) is held completely separate during development and is touched exactly once: after all hyperparameter tuning, feature engineering, and model architecture decisions are finalized on the training and validation sets. Final metrics (ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, calibration, sensitivity/specificity curves) are computed on the test set for both the RNN and baseline. This ensures an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Confounding by patient demographics (age, socioeconomic status proxy): (1) Both the RNN and baseline models include demographic features as explicit inputs, allowing the models to account for these factors; (2) stratification during train/val/test splits ensures demographic balance across splits; (3) subgroup analysis on the test set (stratified by age quintiles and socioeconomic status quartiles) will verify that the RNN's advantage (if present) holds across demographic strata and is not driven by imbalanced baseline compositions; (4) if the RNN outperforms the baseline significantly in some demographics but not others, this signals that the advantage is not robust and may reflect confounding. Confounding by temporal trends: the temporal split naturally controls for drift—both models are evaluated on the same held-out future time period, so neither model gains an advantage from knowing future distribution trends. Potential confound of patient severity at admission: both models receive severity-related features (comorbidity count, prior hospitalization frequency), ensuring both can adjust for this; severity is not a confounder if it is causally prior to the visit sequence and captured by these features.

design_narrative:
### Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

#### 1. Objective and Framing

To test whether an LSTM-based recurrent neural network (RNN) outperforms traditional logistic regression in predicting 30-day hospital readmission rates, using real electronic health records from a regional hospital system. The hospital's operational goal is to identify high-risk patients for targeted intervention, so the metric of success is the model's ability to rank patients by readmission risk (ROC-AUC).

#### 2. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses 5 years of EHR data containing 100,000+ patient visits. The outcome label is binary: readmission = 1 if a patient was admitted within 30 calendar days of discharge from an index visit, otherwise 0. Labels are derived from objective hospital admission timestamps. Patients with fewer than one prior visit are excluded to ensure sequence-based features are meaningful. The dataset is expected to exhibit class imbalance (~8–12% readmission rate), which is typical in clinical settings.

#### 3. Temporal Split Strategy

Data are split chronologically to respect the temporal structure of patient sequences and prevent information leakage:
- **Training set:** Visits occurring in months 1–36 (60% of the 5-year timeline)
- **Validation set:** Visits in months 37–48 (20% of timeline)
- **Test set:** Visits in months 49–60 (20% of timeline)

Within each split, visits are stratified by readmission class to maintain balanced class representation. Critically, patient identifiers are respected: all visits from a single patient must fall entirely within one split to prevent patient-level leakage. Rationale: temporal splitting reveals whether models maintain performance on future patient populations, and patient-level separation ensures the models cannot memorize individual patient trajectories.

#### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature sets are constructed to enable fair comparison:

**For both models:** Demographic features (age, sex, socioeconomic status proxy) and clinical summary features are included: comorbidity count (derived from ICD-10 codes), number of chronic conditions, prior 12-month hospitalization count, ED visit frequency, current medication count, length of stay for the index visit, and primary diagnosis code.

**For RNN only:** Visit sequences spanning the preceding 12 months are encoded. Each visit in the sequence is represented as: [admission date, discharge date, LOS, primary/secondary diagnosis codes, procedure codes, medication classes, lab value summaries]. Sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed length of 12 visits.

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders) are fit exclusively on the training set and then applied deterministically to validation and test sets. This prevents the validation and test sets from influencing feature normalization, encoding choices, or vocabulary construction.

#### 5. Model and Baseline

**Primary model (RNN):** A stacked LSTM-based RNN with embedding layers for clinical codes (embedding dimension 64–128), two LSTM layers (64–128 hidden units, dropout 0.3–0.5), an attention mechanism to weight informative visits, and a dense layer concatenating attention output with demographic features, followed by a sigmoid output.

**Baseline (Logistic Regression):** Linear logistic regression trained on the same demographic and clinical summary features as the RNN (excluding raw sequences). Regularization strength (L2, hyperparameter C) is tuned via cross-validation on the training set.

Both models receive equivalent tuning effort via hyperparameter search on the validation set. The baseline is a fair comparison because it uses the same data, label, and split strategy, and is not a strawman—it represents established clinical practice for readmission prediction.

#### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set only. For the RNN, embedding dimension, LSTM size, dropout rate, and learning rate are tuned to maximize ROC-AUC on the validation set. For logistic regression, regularization strength C is tuned similarly. The test set is held completely separate and is evaluated exactly once at the end.

#### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: ROC-AUC.** ROC-AUC directly reflects the hospital's operational need: the ability to rank patients by risk. A higher AUC means the model better identifies high-risk patients, enabling targeted intervention. ROC-AUC is robust to class imbalance and does not require committing to a specific probability threshold during evaluation.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC (complementary view under class imbalance), sensitivity at 90% specificity (operationally relevant for false alarm minimization), calibration error and Brier score (critical if the hospital uses absolute risk scores), and F1-score.

#### 8. Controls for Confounding

Key confounds—age, socioeconomic status, and patient severity—are controlled as follows:
- Both models include demographic and severity-related features (comorbidity count, prior hospitalization frequency) as explicit inputs.
- Stratified splits ensure demographic balance across train/val/test sets.
- Subgroup analysis on the test set (stratified by age and socioeconomic status) will verify that any RNN advantage is robust across demographic strata and is not driven by confounding.
- Temporal splits control for distribution drift.

#### 9. Scope of Claims

This design will establish whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression on this specific hospital system's data. It does not establish superiority across different healthcare systems or patient populations without external validation. The design isolates the contribution of modeling approach while controlling for standard clinical features.
